definition: A careful statistical claim states what the data support without extending the conclusion beyond the sample or study design.
A categorical variable is a variable whose values are group labels, categories, or names. A categorical variable places each observational unit into one category or group label.
Data are pieces of information collected about items or individuals.
A statistical statement is written in context when it connects the statistical result to the real-world people, objects, variables, and question being studied.
An investigative question tells what the study is trying to learn from data and should determine the population, variables, study type, and appropriate conclusion.
An observational unit is the individual person, object, animal, or event from which data are collected.
A parameter is a numerical summary or attribute that describes a population.
A population is the entire group of items or individuals we want to learn about. The population should match the group named in the investigative question.
A quantitative variable is a variable whose values are numerical measurements or counts, generally with units.
A sample is the smaller group or subset of the population from which data are actually collected.
Sample size is the number of items or individuals in the sample.
A statistic is a numerical summary or attribute that describes a sample.
The statistical process is the cycle of asking a question, collecting data, analyzing data, and interpreting results.
A statistical question is a question that can be answered with data and anticipates variability in the data.
A statistical study is a study in which data are collected from a sample to answer an investigative question about a larger population.
Variability is the natural difference expected among observations in a data set.
A variable is a characteristic measured or recorded about each observational unit that may vary from one unit to another.
A bar graph displays categorical data by using separate bars to show the frequency or relative frequency for each category.
A careful categorical summary describes common categories, uncommon categories, and important proportions in context.
A categorical distribution describes how the observations are divided among the categories of a categorical variable.
A frequency is the number of observations in a category.
The mode is the category or value with the greatest frequency.
A pie chart displays categorical data by showing each category as a part of the whole.
Center describes a typical or middle value of a distribution.
A cluster is a group of data values that are close together.
A distribution shows the values of a variable and how often they occur.
A dotplot displays quantitative data by placing a dot above each observed value on a number line.
A gap is an interval with no observed data values between parts of a distribution.
A histogram displays quantitative data by grouping values into intervals and using bars to show the frequency or relative frequency in each interval.
An outlier is a data value that stands apart from the overall pattern of the distribution.
A roughly symmetric distribution has two sides that are approximately balanced around the center.
Shape describes the overall pattern of a distribution.
A skewed left distribution has a tail that stretches toward smaller values.
A skewed right distribution has a tail that stretches toward larger values.
Spread describes how much the data values vary.
A stem-and-leaf plot displays quantitative data by separating each value into a stem and a leaf while preserving individual data values.
The 1.5 x IQR rule identifies possible outliers below Q1 - 1.5(IQR) or above Q3 + 1.5(IQR).
A boxplot displays the five-number summary and may show possible outliers.
A comparison of distributions describes similarities and differences in shape, center, spread, and unusual features in context.
The first quartile, Q1, is a value near the 25th percentile of an ordered quantitative data set.
The five-number summary consists of the minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum.
The interquartile range, or IQR, is Q3 minus Q1. The interquartile range is a resistant measure of spread because it focuses on the middle half of the data.
The mean is the arithmetic average of a quantitative data set. The mean is a nonresistant measure of center because it is pulled in the direction of extreme values.
The median is the middle value of an ordered quantitative data set. The median is a resistant measure of center because it is not strongly affected by extreme values.
A percentile describes the percentage of observations at or below a value.
Quartiles divide an ordered quantitative data set into four approximately equal parts.
The range is the maximum value minus the minimum value. Range is a measure of spread.
A resistant measure is a statistic that is not strongly affected by outliers or skewness.
The standard deviation measures the typical distance of data values from the mean. The standard deviation is a nonresistant measure of spread because it is affected by values far from the mean.
The third quartile, Q3, is a value near the 75th percentile of an ordered quantitative data set.
A z-score measures how many standard deviations a value is from the mean.
An appropriate conclusion is a claim that does not go beyond what the study design and data can support.
An association is a relationship between variables in which values of one variable tend to occur with certain values of another variable.
A cause-and-effect conclusion states that changes in one variable caused changes in another variable and requires a well-designed experiment.
A census attempts to collect data from every member of the population.
An experiment imposes treatments in order to measure a response.
A generalization extends a conclusion from a sample to a larger population.
An observational study observes individuals without imposing treatments.
A sample survey collects data from part of a population in order to learn about the whole population.
Study design is the plan for how data will be collected so that the method matches the investigative question.
A cluster sample divides the population into groups, randomly selects some whole groups, and includes all individuals in the selected groups.
A convenience sample selects individuals who are easy to reach.
A random sample uses chance to select individuals from the population.

Random sampling supports generalization by using chance to reduce selection bias in choosing individuals from the population. Random sampling helps determine whether conclusions from a study can be generalized to a larger population.
A representative sample reflects the population well enough to support reasonable conclusions about that population.
A sampling method is the procedure used to select individuals from a population.
A simple random sample gives every possible sample of the same size an equal chance of being selected.
A stratified random sample divides the population into groups with a shared characteristic and randomly selects individuals from every group.
A systematic random sample starts at a randomly chosen point and then selects individuals according to a regular pattern.
A voluntary response sample consists of people who choose themselves to participate.
A leading question is worded in a way that encourages a particular response.
Nonrandom sampling uses a method other than chance to select individuals and limits the ability to make a valid generalization.
Nonresponse bias may occur when people who respond differ in important ways from people who do not respond.
Question wording bias occurs when the wording of a survey question influences the responses.
Response bias occurs when answers are inaccurate because of wording, pressure, memory, embarrassment, dishonesty, or another factor affecting the response.
Sampling bias occurs when the method of selecting the sample tends to favor certain outcomes.
A self-report question asks people to report information about themselves.
Undercoverage bias may occur when part of the population is left out or less likely to be selected by the sampling method.
A valid generalization is a conclusion about a population that is supported by a sampling method likely to represent that population.
Blinding prevents participants, researchers, or both from knowing which treatment was assigned.
Blocking separates variation caused by the blocking variable from other variation so that treatment comparisons are more precise.
A blocking variable is a variable used to form blocks because it is expected to affect the response.
A comparison group is a group used as a reference for judging the effect of another treatment.
A completely randomized design randomly assigns all experimental units to treatments.
A confounding variable is a variable whose effect on the response cannot be separated from the effect of the treatment.
A control group provides a comparison group that does not receive the active treatment or receives a baseline treatment.
Direct control means holding other variables constant when possible.
A double-blind experiment is an experiment in which neither the participants nor the interacting researchers know which treatment was assigned.
An experimental unit is the individual object, animal, or person receiving a treatment.
The explanatory variable is the variable that may explain or cause changes in the response variable.
An extraneous variable is a variable believed to affect the response but not serving as the explanatory variable being studied.
A cause-and-effect conclusion is justified when a well-designed experiment uses random assignment to reduce confounding between the explanatory and response variables.
A matched pairs design compares two treatments within naturally paired units or within the same unit, with treatment assignment or order randomized.
A placebo is an inactive treatment used to account for the placebo effect.
The placebo effect is the difference between the average response to a placebo and the average response to no treatment.
Random assignment uses chance to assign experimental units to treatments. Random assignment supports cause-and-effect conclusions by reducing the potential effect of confounding variables.
A randomized block design groups similar experimental units into blocks before randomly assigning treatments within each block.
Replication means using enough experimental units in each treatment group to reduce the effect of chance variation.
The response variable is the outcome measured in a study or experiment.
A single-blind experiment is an experiment in which either the participants or the interacting researchers do not know which treatment was assigned.
A treatment is a condition imposed on experimental units.
A causal claim states that changes in one variable cause changes in another variable.
A descriptive claim summarizes the data that were actually collected.
An overclaim is a conclusion that is stronger than the study design or data can support.
Random sampling supports generalization to a population, while random assignment supports cause-and-effect conclusions in an experiment.
The strongest claim justified is the conclusion that matches the study design without going beyond what the data collection method supports.
An association exists when the distribution of one variable changes across the categories of another variable.
A conditional distribution describes how the categories of one variable are distributed within a selected category of another variable.
A conditional relative frequency is calculated within a particular row or column group.
A joint frequency is the count of observations in one specific combination of categories for two categorical variables.
A joint relative frequency is a joint frequency divided by the total number of observations.
A marginal frequency is a total for one row or one column in a two-way table.
A marginal relative frequency is a row or column total divided by the total number of observations.
A segmented bar graph displays conditional distributions by dividing each bar into segments that represent relative frequencies within a group.
A side-by-side bar graph displays frequencies or relative frequencies for categories of one variable separately for groups formed by another variable.
A two-way table summarizes data for two categorical variables.
The empirical probability of an event is the proportion of simulated trials in which the event occurs.
An event is a collection of outcomes.
The law of large numbers says that for independent trials, as the number of trials increases, the long-run relative frequency gets closer and closer to a single value.
An outcome is the result of one trial of a random process.
A random process has outcomes that are uncertain in the short run but predictable in the long run.
A simulation imitates a random process using a model such as random digits, cards, dice, coins, physical objects, or technology.
A trial is one repetition of a simulation.

concept: addition rule for mutually exclusive events
definition: If A and B are mutually exclusive, then P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B).
The complement of event A is the event that A does not occur.
The complement rule says P(not A) = 1 - P(A).
Equally likely outcomes have the same chance of occurring.
The intersection of events A and B is the event that both A and B occur.
Two events are mutually exclusive if they cannot occur at the same time.
Probability is a number between 0 and 1 that describes how likely an event is to occur.
A sample space is the set of all possible nonoverlapping outcomes of a random process.
A conditional probability is the probability of an event given that another event has occurred.
The general addition rule says P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B).
The general multiplication rule says P(A and B) = P(A)P(B | A).
Two events are independent if knowing that one event occurred does not change the probability of the other event.
P(A | B) means the probability of event A given that event B has occurred.
The union of events A and B is the event that A occurs, B occurs, or both occur.
A discrete random variable has a countable set of possible values.
Expected value is the long-run average value of a random variable over many repetitions.
A probability distribution gives the possible values of a random variable and their probabilities.
A random variable assigns a numerical value to each outcome of a random process.

concept: standard deviation of a random variable
definition: The standard deviation of a random variable measures the typical distance of values from the expected value.
A binomial distribution gives the probabilities for the possible numbers of successes in a binomial setting.
For X binomial with n trials and probability p, P(X = k) = C(n,k)p^k(1-p)^(n-k).
A binomial random variable counts the number of successes in n independent trials with the same probability of success on each trial.
A binomial setting has a fixed number of trials, two possible outcomes on each trial, independent trials, and the same probability of success on each trial.
A failure is any outcome that is not the success in a binomial setting.
A success is the outcome being counted in a binomial setting.
The 68-95-99.7 rule says that in an approximately normal distribution, about 68%, 95%, and 99.7% of values are within 1, 2, and 3 standard deviations of the mean, respectively.
For a continuous random variable, probability is represented by area under the curve.
A continuous random variable can take values along an interval.
A density curve is a curve whose total area is 1 and whose areas represent probabilities.
A normal distribution is bell-shaped and symmetric and is described by its mean and standard deviation.
The standard normal distribution is the normal distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1.
The center of a sampling distribution tells whether the statistic tends to estimate the parameter correctly.
The Central Limit Theorem says that for large random samples, the sampling distribution of sample means is approximately normal, even if the population distribution is not normal.
A sampling distribution is the distribution of a statistic from many random samples of the same size.
The standard error is the standard deviation of a sampling distribution.
The variability of a sampling distribution describes how much the statistic changes from sample to sample.

concept: approximately normal sampling distribution of p-hat
definition: The sampling distribution of p-hat is approximately normal when the Large Counts condition is met.
Bias is a systematic tendency for an estimator to overestimate or underestimate the parameter.

concept: center of the sampling distribution of p-hat
definition: The center of the sampling distribution of p-hat is the population proportion p.
An estimator is a statistic used to estimate a population parameter.
A good estimator has low bias and low variability.
The Large Counts condition for a sample proportion requires both np >= 10 and n(1 - p) >= 10.
The symbol p represents the population proportion.
The symbol p-hat represents the sample proportion.
A point estimate is a single-number estimate of a population parameter.
A population proportion is the true proportion of individuals in a population with a certain characteristic.
A sample proportion is the proportion of individuals in a sample with a certain characteristic and is often used to estimate a population proportion.

concept: sampling distribution of a sample proportion
definition: A sampling distribution of a sample proportion is the distribution of p-hat values from many random samples of the same size.
Sampling variability is the natural variation in a statistic from sample to sample.
The standard deviation of p-hat is sqrt(p(1 - p)/n) when the 10% condition is met.
A surprising sample proportion is a p-hat value that would be unlikely to occur by chance if a claimed population proportion were true.
The variability of an estimator describes how much the statistic changes from sample to sample.
A confidence interval for a proportion requires random data, the 10% condition, and the Large Counts condition.
A confidence interval gives a range of plausible values for a population parameter.
A confidence level describes the long-run success rate of the interval method.
A margin of error describes how far the estimate extends on either side.
A one-proportion z-interval estimates a population proportion p.
A claim is plausible if the claimed value is inside the confidence interval.
The alternative hypothesis states the direction or type of evidence being tested.
A hypothesis test evaluates evidence against a claim about a parameter.
The null hypothesis states the claim being tested, usually a statement of no change or no difference.
A one-proportion z-test evaluates a claim about a population proportion p.
A p-value is the probability, assuming the null hypothesis is true, of getting a result as extreme as or more extreme than the observed result.
The significance level is the cutoff for deciding whether results are statistically significant.
A result is statistically significant if the p-value is less than the significance level.
A test statistic measures how far the sample statistic is from the null value in standard error units.
A smaller alpha reduces the chance of a Type I error but can increase the chance of a Type II error.
A false alarm is an ordinary-language description of a Type I error.
A missed detection is an ordinary-language description of a Type II error.
Power is the probability that a test correctly rejects a false null hypothesis.
A Type I error occurs when we reject a true null hypothesis.
A Type II error occurs when we fail to reject a false null hypothesis.
A two-proportion interval requires random data, independent samples or groups, the 10% condition, and the Large Counts condition for both groups.
The difference of population proportions is the parameter p1 - p2.
A difference of sample proportions estimates a difference of population proportions.

concept: standard error for a two-proportion interval
definition: The standard error for a two-proportion interval uses the two sample proportions separately.
A two-proportion z-interval estimates the difference between two population proportions.
If 0 is in a confidence interval for a difference, 0 is a plausible value for the difference.
The observed difference in sample proportions is p-hat1 - p-hat2.
The pooled proportion combines successes from both groups under the assumption that the null hypothesis is true.

concept: standard error for a two-proportion test
definition: The standard error for a two-proportion z-test uses the pooled proportion.
A two-proportion z-test evaluates a claim about p1 - p2.
The chi-square statistic adds the contributions (observed - expected)^2 / expected across cells.
A chi-square test compares observed counts to expected counts.
A chi-square test for homogeneity compares the distribution of one categorical variable across two or more populations or treatments.
A chi-square test for independence tests whether two categorical variables are associated in one population.

concept: degrees of freedom for a two-way table
definition: The degrees of freedom for a two-way table are (rows - 1)(columns - 1).
Expected counts are the counts we would expect if the null hypothesis were true.
Observed counts are the actual counts from the data.
The 10% condition requires the population size to be at least 10 times the sample size when sampling without replacement.

concept: approximately normal sampling distribution of x-bar
definition: The sampling distribution of x-bar is approximately normal when the population distribution is normal or when the sample size is sufficiently large.
Increasing the sample size decreases the variability of the sampling distribution of x-bar.
The large sample condition says that if n >= 30, the sampling distribution of x-bar can often be modeled approximately by a normal distribution.

concept: mean of the sampling distribution of x-bar
definition: The mean of the sampling distribution of x-bar is equal to the population mean mu when the sampled values are independent.
The normal population condition says that if the population distribution is normal, then the sampling distribution of x-bar is normal for any sample size.
A normal probability graph is a visual display used to judge whether data are reasonably consistent with a normal distribution.
The population mean, mu, is the true mean value of a quantitative variable for the entire population.
The population standard deviation, sigma, measures the variability of individual values in the population.
The randomization condition requires that the data come from a random sample or randomized experiment, as appropriate.
The sample mean, x-bar, is the mean of the quantitative values in one sample.

concept: sampling distribution of a sample mean
definition: A sampling distribution of a sample mean is the distribution of x-bar values from many random samples of the same size.
The standard deviation of x-bar is sigma / sqrt(n) when the sampled values are independent.
For paired data, we analyze the differences, not the two original lists separately.
A t-interval requires random data, the 10% condition when sampling without replacement, and an approximately normal sampling distribution.

concept: degrees of freedom for one-sample or paired t
definition: The degrees of freedom for a one-sample or paired t-procedure are usually n - 1.
A one-sample t-interval estimates a population mean.
A paired design produces two related measurements for each individual or matched pair.
A paired t-interval estimates a population mean difference.
The t-distribution is used when the population standard deviation is unknown.
A t-interval estimates a population mean or a mean difference.
The null hypothesis states a claimed value for a population mean or population mean difference.
A one-sample t-test evaluates a claim about a population mean.
A paired t-test evaluates a claim about a population mean difference.
For paired data, the hypothesis test is performed on the differences.
A t test statistic measures how far the sample mean is from the null value in standard error units.

concept: degrees of freedom for two-sample t
definition: The degrees of freedom for a two-sample t-procedure are found by technology or by a conservative method.
The difference of population means is the parameter mu1 - mu2.
A difference of sample means estimates a difference of population means.
Independent samples are samples whose observations are not paired or linked across groups.

concept: standard error for a two-sample t-interval
definition: The standard error for a two-sample t-interval uses the sample standard deviations and sample sizes from both groups.
A two-sample t-interval estimates the difference between two population means.
The null hypothesis for a two-sample t-test often states mu1 - mu2 = 0.
A small p-value gives evidence against the null hypothesis.
The two-sample t statistic compares the observed difference in sample means to the null difference.
A two-sample t-test evaluates a claim about the difference between two population means.
Bivariate quantitative data consist of paired observations from two quantitative variables measured on the same individuals or units.
Describing a scatterplot means identifying form, direction, strength, and unusual features in context.
The form describes the overall shape of the relationship in a scatterplot.
A linear relationship follows an approximately straight-line pattern in a scatterplot.
A moderate association has points that somewhat follow the overall pattern but still show noticeable scatter.
A negative association means that larger values of the explanatory variable tend to occur with smaller values of the response variable.
A nonlinear relationship follows a curved or other non-straight pattern in a scatterplot.
An ordered pair records the two quantitative values measured for one observational unit.
A positive association means that larger values of the explanatory variable tend to occur with larger values of the response variable.
A scatterplot displays the relationship between two quantitative variables by plotting each observational unit as a point.
The strength describes how closely the points in a scatterplot follow the overall pattern.
A strong association has points that closely follow the overall pattern.
A weak association has points that are widely scattered around the overall pattern.
Correlation describes the strength and direction of the linear association between two quantitative variables.
The correlation coefficient, r, is a unit-free number between -1 and 1 that summarizes the strength and direction of a linear association between two quantitative variables.
Correlation does not imply causation means that a linear association between two variables does not by itself prove that changes in one variable cause changes in the other.
A linear model caution is the reminder that a correlation coefficient close to -1 or 1 does not automatically mean that a linear model is appropriate.
A perfect linear association occurs when r = 1 or r = -1 and all points fall exactly on a straight-line pattern.
Extrapolation means using a model to predict outside the range of observed data.
A least-squares regression line is the line that minimizes the sum of squared residuals.
The predicted response, written y-hat, is the value predicted by a regression model.
A prediction uses the regression line to estimate a response value.
A regression line is a model used to predict a response variable from an explanatory variable.
The slope tells the predicted change in the response variable for a one-unit increase in the explanatory variable.
The y-intercept is the predicted response when the explanatory variable equals 0.
A negative residual means the observed value is below the regression line.
Changing spread in a residual plot may suggest nonconstant variability.
A positive residual means the observed value is above the regression line.
A residual is the difference between the observed response and the predicted response.
The formula for a residual is observed - predicted.
A residual plot displays residuals against the explanatory variable.
A curved pattern in a residual plot suggests that a linear model may not be appropriate.
The coefficient of determination, r-squared, gives the percent of variation in the response variable explained by the linear model using the explanatory variable.
A high r-squared does not prove causation.
Regression output often includes the equation, r, r-squared, and residual information.
The standard deviation of the residuals measures the typical size of prediction errors.
A useful regression model should have a reasonable scatterplot, a random residual plot, and meaningful interpretations.
